questions.question: What is the correct HTML5 doctype declaration?
questions.explanation: The HTML5 doctype is simply <!DOCTYPE html>. It is case-insensitive and much shorter than legacy doctypes used in HTML 4 and XHTML.

questions.question: What happens when a browser cannot find a valid doctype declaration?
choices: The browser uses HTML5 by default
questions.explanation: Without a valid doctype, browsers fall back to quirks mode, which emulates older browser behavior and can cause inconsistent rendering across browsers.

questions.question: Which rendering mode does a valid HTML5 doctype trigger in browsers?
questions.explanation: A valid <!DOCTYPE html> declaration tells the browser to render the page in standards mode, which follows the W3C specifications for layout and rendering.

questions.question: Where must the doctype declaration be placed in an HTML document?
choices: Before the <html> tag, as the very first line
questions.explanation: The doctype declaration must appear at the very beginning of the document, before the <html> tag. It tells the browser which version of HTML to use for rendering.

questions.question: When no doctype is present, browsers render the page in ___ mode.
questions.explanation: Quirks mode causes the browser to emulate legacy behavior from older browsers, which can lead to inconsistent rendering of CSS box models and other features.

questions.question: The HTML5 doctype is ___ , meaning it works regardless of uppercase or lowercase letters.
questions.explanation: <!DOCTYPE html>, <!doctype html>, and <!Doctype Html> are all valid. The HTML5 doctype is case-insensitive by specification.
